source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
title: Prefer using dedicated EKS Service Accounts

input_row.source: CIS AMAZON ELASTIC KUBERNETES SERVICE (EKS) BENCHMARK V1.6.0
input_row.title: Prefer using dedicated EKS Service Accounts
input_row.description: Kubernetes workloads should not use cluster node service accounts to authenticate to Amazon EKS APIs. Each Kubernetes workload that needs to authenticate to other AWS services using AWS IAM should be provisioned with a dedicated Service account.
input_row.rationale: Manual approaches for authenticating Kubernetes workloads running on Amazon EKS against AWS APIs are: storing service account keys as a Kubernetes secret (which introduces manual key rotation and potential for key compromise); or use of the underlying nodes' IAM Service account, which violates the principle of least privilege on a multi-tenanted node, when one pod needs to have access to a service, but every other pod on the node that uses the Service account does not.
input_row.audit: For each namespace in the cluster, review the rights assigned to the default service account and ensure that it has no roles or cluster roles bound to it apart from the defaults. Additionally ensure that the automountServiceAccountToken: false setting is in place for each default service account.
input_row.remediation: With IAM roles for service accounts on Amazon EKS clusters, you can associate an IAM role with a Kubernetes service account. This service account can then provide AWS permissions to the containers in any pod that uses that service account. With this feature, you no longer need to provide extended permissions to the worker node IAM role so that pods on that node can call AWS APIs. Applications must sign their AWS API requests with AWS credentials. This feature provides a strategy for managing credentials for your applications, similar to the way that Amazon EC2 instance profiles provide credentials to Amazon EC2 instances. Instead of creating and distributing your AWS credentials to the containers or using the Amazon EC2 instance’s role, you can associate an IAM role with a Kubernetes service account. The applications in the pod’s containers can then use an AWS SDK or the AWS CLI to make API requests to authorized AWS services. The IAM roles for service accounts feature provides the following benefits: • Least privilege — By using the IAM roles for service accounts feature, you no longer need to provide extended permissions to the worker node IAM role so that pods on that node can call AWS APIs. You can scope IAM permissions to a service account, and only pods that use that service account have access to those permissions. This feature also eliminates the need for third-party solutions such as kiam or kube2iam. • Credential isolation — A container can only retrieve credentials for the IAM role that is associated with the service account to which it belongs. A container never has access to credentials that are intended for another container that belongs to another pod. • Audit-ability — Access and event logging is available through CloudTrail to help ensure retrospective auditing. To get started, see list text hereEnabling IAM roles for service accounts on your cluster. For an end-to-end walkthrough using eksctl, see Walkthrough: Updating a DaemonSet to use IAM for service accounts. References: 1. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/iam-roles-for-service- accounts.html 2. https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/iam-roles-for-service- accounts-cni-walkthrough.html 3. https://aws.github.io/aws-eks-best-practices/security/docs/iam/#scope-the-iam- role-trust-policy-for-irsa-to-the-service-account-name

step1_initial_assessment:
**APPROACH DECISION:** Manual

DECISION CRITERIA:
- The audit specifics involve verifying the permissions and configurations of Kubernetes service accounts within an EKS cluster, particularly ensuring the `automountServiceAccountToken: false` setting and checking roles bound to default service accounts. This kind of verification requires understanding the context of roles and permissions within Kubernetes namespaces more deeply than what AWS API directly exposes.
- While AWS provides APIs and tools to manage IAM roles and policies, the verification of specific Kubernetes resource configurations and understanding their implications often necessitates human judgment and visual inspection through the Kubernetes command-line interface (kubectl) or similar tools.
- Although IAM roles and policies can be managed and audited using AWS APIs, ensuring compliance to this control involves inspecting Kubernetes-specific configurations which typically require a manual check.
- This task involves confirming application-level security practices (i.e., how the service accounts are used within the pods), which goes beyond AWS and into the operational practices within Kubernetes, demanding manual intervention.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. **Connect to the Kubernetes Cluster:** Use kubectl to ensure connectivity to the EKS cluster.
   - Command: `kubectl config use-context <your-cluster-name>`

2. **List All Service Accounts in Each Namespace:**
   - Command: `kubectl get serviceaccounts --all-namespaces`
   - Verify that service accounts dedicated to specific applications are used and not cluster node service accounts.

3. **Check Permissions of Default Service Account:**
   - Command: `kubectl get serviceaccounts default -n <namespace> -o yaml`
   - Verify no roles or cluster roles are bound to the default service account beyond the permissions needed.

4. **Ensure `automountServiceAccountToken` is False:**
   - Command: `kubectl get serviceaccounts default -n <namespace> -o yaml`
   - Check if `automountServiceAccountToken` is set to false unless explicitly required and justified.

5. **Inspect IAM Role Associations:**
   - Command: `kubectl describe serviceaccount <service-account-name> -n <namespace>`
   - Confirm IAM roles are properly associated with service accounts as specified by your IAM configuration strategies.

6. **Verification of AWS Relations:**
   - Use AWS IAM console or CLI to ensure the IAM role associated with the Kubernetes service account has the least privilege needed for AWS service calls.
   - Command (CLI example for role policies): `aws iam list-attached-role-policies --role-name <role-name>`

These steps require interpretation and validation within the specific context of your cluster setup and organizational policies, which makes the process primarily manual and context-sensitive.
